label: Momentum Definition
claim: Momentum is a physical quantity that includes both mass and velocity, characterizing the "quantity of motion" of an object.
evidence: Like kinetic energy, this quantity includes both mass and velocity; like kinetic energy, it is a way of characterizing the “quantity of motion” of an object.

label: Momentum Formula
claim: The momentum (p) of an object is the product of its mass (m) and its velocity (v), represented by the formula p = mv.
evidence: The momentum p of an object is the product of its mass and its velocity: p=mv.

label: Momentum Type
claim: Momentum is a vector quantity, just like velocity.
evidence: As shown in Figure 9.2, momentum is a vector quantity (since velocity is).

label: Momentum vs. Kinetic Energy
claim: Unlike kinetic energy, momentum depends equally on an object’s mass and velocity.
evidence: Unlike kinetic energy, momentum depends equally on an object’s mass and velocity.

label: Momentum Usefulness
claim: Momentum is most useful when determining whether an object’s motion is difficult or easy to change over a short time interval.
evidence: It is perhaps most useful when determining whether an object’s motion is difficult to change (Figure 9.3) or easy to change (Figure 9.4) over a short time interval.

label: Momentum Definition
claim: Momentum is calculated by multiplying an object's mass by its velocity (momentum = mass × velocity).
evidence: its momentum is thus pmolecule=(6×10−25kg)(500ms)=3×10−22kg·ms.

label: Molecular Momentum Example
claim: The momentum of an air molecule at room temperature is 3×10−22 kg·ms.
evidence: its momentum is thus pmolecule=(6×10−25kg)(500ms)=3×10−22kg·ms.

label: Car Momentum Example
claim: A typical automobile with a speed of 15 m/s and a mass of 1400 kg has a momentum of 21,000 kg·ms.

label: Momentum Scale Comparison
claim: The momentum of a molecule and a car are vastly different, differing by 27 orders of magnitude (a factor of a billion billion billion).
evidence: These momenta are different by 27 orders of magnitude, or a factor of a billion billion billion!

label: Linear Momentum
claim: Linear momentum is a physical quantity.
evidence: Linear momentum is a physical quantity.

label: Momentum (Conceptual)
claim: Momentum is related to mass and velocity.
evidence: Linear momentum is the product of mass and velocity.

label: Momentum Calculation
claim: The formula for linear momentum is mass times velocity.
evidence: Linear momentum is the product of mass and velocity.

label: Momentum Definition
claim: Momentum is a physics term that refers to the quantity of motion that an object has.
evidence: Momentum is a physics term; it refers to the quantity of motion that an object has.

label: Momentum as Mass in Motion
claim: Momentum can be defined as 'mass in motion'.
evidence: Momentum can be defined as "mass in motion*."

label: Condition for Momentum
claim: If an object is in motion (on the move), then it has momentum.
evidence: If an object is in motion (on the move) then it has momentum.

label: Momentum Formula
claim: The momentum of an object is equal to the mass of the object times the velocity of the object (p = m • v).
evidence: Momentum = mass • velocity

label: Momentum Proportionality
claim: Momentum is directly proportional to an object's mass and directly proportional to the object's velocity.
evidence: The equation illustrates that momentum is directly proportional to an object's mass and directly proportional to the object's velocity.

label: Sports Analogy for Momentum
claim: In sports, a team that has momentum is on the move and is going to take some effort to stop.
evidence: A team that has the momentum is on the move and is going to take some effort to stop.

label: Momentum Units
claim: The standard metric unit for momentum is kg•m/s.
evidence: The standard metric unit of momentum is the kg•m/s.

label: Momentum Unit
claim: The standard metric unit of momentum is kg•m/s, but other units like kg•mi/hr, kg•km/hr, and g•cm/s are also acceptable.
evidence: While the kg•m/s is the standard metric unit of momentum, there are a variety of other units that are acceptable (though not conventional) units of momentum. Examples include kg•mi/hr, kg•km/hr, and g•cm/s.

label: Momentum as a Vector Quantity
claim: Momentum is a vector quantity, meaning it is fully described by both magnitude and direction.
evidence: Momentum is a vector quantity. As discussed in an earlier unit, a vector quantity is a quantity that is fully described by both magnitude and direction.

label: Momentum Direction
claim: The direction of the momentum vector is the same as the direction of the velocity of the ball.
evidence: The direction of the momentum vector is the same as the direction of the velocity of the ball.

label: Large Momentum Condition
claim: An object has large momentum if both its mass and its velocity are large.
evidence: it becomes obvious that an object has a large momentum if both its mass and its velocity are large.

label: Momentum of an Object at Rest
claim: Objects at rest do not have momentum.
evidence: Objects at rest do not have momentum - they do not have any " mass in motion."

label: Momentum Equation
claim: The momentum equation is p = m*v.
evidence: p = m*v

label: Momentum Relationship (Mass)
claim: A doubling of the mass results in a doubling of the momentum when velocity is constant.
evidence: A doubling of the mass results in a doubling of the momentum.

label: Momentum Relationship (Velocity)
claim: A quadrupling in velocity results in a quadrupling of the momentum when mass is constant.
evidence: A quadrupling in velocity results in a quadrupling of the momentum.

label: Momentum as a Guide
claim: The momentum equation serves as a 'guide to thinking' and not merely a 'plug-and-chug recipe for algebraic problem-solving.'
evidence: p = m*v serves as a "guide to thinking" and not merely a "plug-and-chug recipe for algebraic problem-solving."

label: Velocity
claim: Velocity is related to distance and time; the tight end travels twice the distance of the lineman in the same time, making the tight end twice as fast.
evidence: The tight end travels twice the distance of the lineman in the same amount of time. Thus, the tight end is twice as fast (vtight end = 6 m/s).

label: Momentum
claim: Momentum is a quantity that can be compared between objects, and the halfback and tight end have the greatest momentum, with both having the same amount.
evidence: Both the halfback and the tight end have the greatest momentum. The each have the same amount of momentum - 540 kg*m/s.

label: Momentum Calculation
claim: Momentum is calculated by multiplying mass by velocity (kg*m/s).
evidence: The lineman only has 360 kg*m/s.

label: Momentum Comparison
claim: The halfback and tight end have the same amount of momentum (540 kg*m/s), which is greater than the lineman's momentum (360 kg*m/s).
evidence: Both the halfback and the tight end have the greatest momentum. The each have the same amount of momentum - 540 kg*m/s. The lineman only has 360 kg*m/s.

claim: Momentum is related to topics like Work and Energy and Circular Motion.

label: Momentum
claim: Momentum is a quantity possessed by a car, with an example of a car possessing 20,000 units of momentum.
evidence: A car possesses 20 000 units of momentum.

label: Velocity and Momentum Relationship (Velocity)
claim: If a car's velocity is doubled, its momentum will also be doubled.
evidence: p = 40 000 units (doubling the velocity will double the momentum)

label: Mass and Momentum Relationship (Mass)
claim: If a car's mass is doubled, its momentum will also be doubled.
evidence: p = 40 000 units (doubling the mass will double the momentum)

label: Combined Effect on Momentum
claim: If both the velocity and the mass of an object are doubled, its momentum is quadrupled (doubled twice).
evidence: p = 80 000 units (doubling the velocity will double the momentum and doubling the mass will also double the momentum; the combined result is that the momentum is doubled twice -quadrupled)
